narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, an LSAT score of 28, lower-middle family income, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 47% chance that you would fail the bar exam—high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your application. This decision hinged on just a few key factors from your profile that stood out the most to the model. The strongest influence pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, well below the typical student's average of around 5.5, signaling to the model a notable dip in later academic performance compared to peers. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, which exceeds the average of 3.21, worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward bar exam success. Similarly, your lower-middle family income bracket contributed positively, as the model associates such backgrounds with resilience and drive in many successful cases. While your LSAT score of 28 is lower than the average of 36.7 and your full-time status aligns with most students, these played smaller roles here. Overall, your Year 3 performance was the dominant factor tipping the scales toward rejection, outweighing your solid undergrad record and background. Many admitted students have stronger consistent decile rankings across years, placing you below that typical profile despite your strengths. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Hispanic leader in law—it's a data-driven risk assessment. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying next cycle with updated credentials that address the model's main concern. You have the foundation; refining these areas could shift your predicted odds dramatically. Keep pushing—many students in similar situations have turned rejections into success stories.
